html:
Net Protections Holdings, TSE:7383

Net Protections is not the broken, loss-making BNPL story implied by its post-IPO chart. It is now a profitable Japanese payments infrastructure company with two underappreciated growth engines, NP Kakebarai for B2B invoice/payment outsourcing and atone for app-based consumer BNPL, sitting on top of a mature cash-generating NP Atobarai base.

The stale thesis was directionally right that B2B is important, but wrong on several facts. The product is NP Kakebarai, not “NP POSTER.” The stock is not around ¥1,150, it last closed at ¥437 on May 15, 2026. The share count is roughly 99.4 million, not 33 million. The company is not currently loss-making. FY3/26 operating profit rose 35.4% to ¥2.85 billion, net income rose 28.3% to ¥1.73 billion, and management guides FY3/27 operating profit to ¥3.6 billion and EPS to ¥22.01 in the latest results presentation.

The variant perception is simpler and cleaner than the old draft: this is a small, ignored, complicated Japanese fintech where the headline FY3/27 P/E of about 20x hides a business already generating cash, already profitable, and targeting ¥6.0 billion of operating profit by FY3/29. At today’s roughly ¥43.5 billion market cap, that is about 10x my estimate of FY3/29 after-tax earnings if management executes, or materially cheaper on EV because the company had ¥20.2 billion of cash and only ¥6.0 billion of short-term borrowings at March 2026 with no long-term debt.

The balance sheet is the biggest misunderstanding. Investors see ¥64.0 billion of liabilities and treat the company like a levered lender. But the key liabilities are merchant settlement payables. Net Protections had ¥54.3 billion of operating payables matched against roughly ¥50.5 billion of gross receivables, and management explicitly says the business runs with a negative cash conversion cycle and limited rate sensitivity. This is not Affirm-style consumer funding leverage. The real risk is credit losses and merchant concentration, not a debt stack.

FY3/26 shows the transition clearly. Total GMV grew 19.1% to ¥764.4 billion. Mature NP Atobarai/other GMV barely grew, but still produced ¥8.0 billion of gross profit. That is the cash cow. atone GMV grew 55.4% to ¥62.1 billion and gross profit grew 81.0% to ¥878 million. NP Kakebarai GMV grew 38.5% to ¥343.9 billion and gross profit grew 32.7% to ¥3.15 billion. The business is no longer a single consumer postpay product. It is a portfolio where the old product funds the growth products.

The B2B asset is especially interesting because Japan’s invoice workflow remains manual, fragmented, and labor constrained. NP Kakebarai handles credit screening, invoice issuance, collection, dunning, and default risk. The company says about 850,000 annual purchasing companies use it, equal to roughly one in five Japanese companies, and claims more than 70% share in B2B kakebarai service volume. Its investor deck frames the broader B2B opportunity at ¥226 trillion, while the narrower outsourced B2B kakebarai market is expected to compound around 27% per year based on Yano Research data cited by the company.

This is not a high-take-rate payment story. B2B gross profit was only 0.92% of FY3/26 GMV, far below the mature B2C gross-profit rate. That is why the market can miss it. Large enterprise volumes can dilute take rate, and Q4 B2B gross profit rose only 13.5% on 31.0% GMV growth. But the right question is not whether B2B has software margins today. The right question is whether Net Protections becomes the embedded risk and billing layer for high-volume Japanese service verticals where back-office friction is expensive. Timee, GO BUSINESS, recruiting, spot work, wholesale, SaaS, office furniture, and liquor distribution are exactly the kinds of verticals where invoice operations scale poorly inside the merchant and can scale well inside Net Protections.

atone is the second torque point. It is still small, but management’s plan implies roughly 60% GMV CAGR through FY3/29, driven by non-physical-goods e-commerce, PSP and cart integrations, and atone plus installment functionality. This introduces more consumer credit risk than B2B, but it also gives Net Protections a path to rebuild consumer BNPL growth without depending on the legacy NP Atobarai checkout footprint. In Q4, atone gross profit rose 206% year over year, albeit from a small base. If that line starts contributing real profit, the company will stop screening as an ex-growth payment processor.

The forward valuation is where the setup becomes asymmetric. FY3/27 guidance is GMV ¥890 billion, operating revenue ¥27.8 billion, gross profit ¥13.6 billion, operating profit ¥3.6 billion, EBITDA ¥5.6 billion, and EPS ¥22.01. At ¥437, the stock is about 19.9x FY3/27 EPS. That is not obviously optically cheap. But the three-year plan calls for FY3/29 GMV of ¥1.4 trillion and operating profit of ¥6.0 billion, with a longer-term target of ¥2.0 trillion GMV and ¥10.0 billion operating profit. Taxed at roughly 30%, FY3/29 operating profit becomes about ¥4.1 billion of net income, or roughly ¥41 per share. Today’s price is about 10.5x that earnings power for a business growing operating profit at a planned 28% CAGR.

Cash flow gives the stock additional support. FY3/26 operating cash flow was ¥9.0 billion and capex/intangible investment was about ¥1.7 billion, so reported free cash flow was over ¥7 billion. Some of that is settlement timing and should not be valued like permanent owner earnings, but even on a more conservative pre-working-capital basis, the company is no longer consuming capital. The old “BNPL cash burn” narrative is stale.

The retail context also helps the variant view. Yahoo Finance Japan message-board chatter around 7383 is heavily short-term and technical, with users talking about monthly moving-average resistance and trading around shorts rather than underwriting FY3/29 earnings power. Meanwhile, Japanese analyst consensus screens at roughly ¥715, and TipRanks flagged a recent buy rating with a ¥900 target. This is not a hidden stock, but it is not yet owned or discussed like a compounding B2B payment infrastructure name.

The key risk is that the medium-term plan is ambitious. B2B volume could keep growing while economics deteriorate if large merchants demand lower pricing. atone plus could raise credit losses just as the company increases debt-funded installment exposure. No dividend or buyback means shareholders are relying on execution and rerating. And if Japanese SME failures rise, even a negative-CCC model can see credit costs and collection costs hit gross profit quickly.

But the downside is not a zero. The mature NP Atobarai base still generates substantial gross profit. The company has net cash against financial debt. FY3/27 guidance already embeds another profit step-up. Book value is ¥211 per share, and while tangible book is much lower after goodwill and software, the operating franchise is now profitable enough that the stock should not be valued like distressed fintech equity.

Bear case: ¥300, down 31%. B2B growth proves low quality, credit costs rise, atone installment expansion gets treated as consumer credit risk, and the market values the company at roughly 13x depressed ¥23 EPS or a modest premium to book.

Base case: ¥720, up 65%. Net Protections hits or comes close to the FY3/29 plan, earns roughly ¥40 to ¥42 per share, and the market applies 17x to a profitable Japanese payments platform with B2B leadership and improving operating leverage.

Bull case: ¥1,100, up 152%. B2B keeps compounding near 25%, atone becomes a real profit contributor, operating profit moves toward ¥8 billion before the long-term ¥10 billion goal, and the stock rerates to about 20x mid-cycle EPS of ¥55 as investors stop treating it like a failed BNPL IPO.

At ¥437, the stock is not cheap on stale trailing optics or simple FY3/27 P/E. It is cheap if the FY3/29 profit bridge is real, if B2B gross-profit leakage is contained, and if the market eventually values Net Protections as a Japanese invoice-risk and payment workflow platform rather than a consumer BNPL relic.
